justification: All six pass criteria met: (1) Scheduling lag metric gap identified as primary SLO-monitoring gap — called out as 'The Fundamental Gap' and the single highest-priority fix. (2) Retry tracking gap identified — marked as 'broken' in integration assessment with no visibility into retry state. (3) Current alert critiqued as symptom-based — explicitly noted that the error count alert is 'a proxy at best' and contrasted with SLO-based alerting. (4) Specific metric recommendations provided: renewal_scheduling_lag_seconds histogram, renewal_retry_attempts_total counter, plus additional metrics. (5) SLO-based alerting recommended with p99 threshold on scheduling lag and burn-rate style alerts. (6) Verdict is NOT READY.

observations.text: Exceptionally thorough analysis — goes well beyond minimum criteria with queue depth, dead scheduler detection, and poison message scenarios
Excellent framing of the core problem: 'You have an SLO that you cannot measure' — immediately communicates business impact
Correctly identifies that the uninstrumented boundaries (scheduler→processor, processor→retry) are more dangerous than the instrumented one (billing API)
Provides actionable must-have metrics table with types and rationale
Identifies the silent scheduler death as the highest-risk scenario — a subtle and critical insight
Retry tracking gap thoroughly covered: queue depth, attempt count, exhaustion counter, and inability to distinguish initial vs retry errors
Appropriate escalation routing to other agents for business impact, implementation, and architecture decisions
